edge: PointConvFormer is a building block for point cloud-based deep network architectures.
PointConvFormer combines ideas from point convolution and Transformers.
In PointConvFormer, attention computed from feature difference between points in the neighborhood is used to modify the convolutional weights at each point.
Attention helps to select relevant points in the neighborhood for convolution.
PointConvFormer is suitable for tasks like segmentation at the point level.
PointConvFormer is suitable for tasks like scene flow estimation at the point level.
Results show that PointConvFormer offers a better accuracy-speed tradeoff than classic convolutions, regular transformers, and voxelized sparse convolution approaches.
Visualizations show that PointConvFormer performs similarly to convolution on flat areas, whereas the neighborhood selection effect is stronger on object boundaries.

Both are representations of the input data, but voxel-based and point-based models have different approaches. The need for fusion between sparse voxel and point-based models is questioned due to the same information input. Fusion adds complexity and memory usage to the model.

The shape of the kNN neighborhood varies in different parts of the point cloud. Including irrelevant points, noise, and background in the neighborhood, especially around object boundaries, can negatively impact the performance and robustness of point-based models.
The generalization theory of CNNs suggests that including points with significant feature correlation in the same neighborhood can improve the robustness of models with kNN neighborhoods.
Filtering out irrelevant neighbors in a kNN neighborhood can be achieved by using feature correlation. This filtering process makes subsequent convolutions more generalizable.
PointConvFormer introduces a method to compute attention weights based on feature differences and use them to reweight points in the neighborhood of a point-based convolutional model.
PointConvFormer combines features in the neighborhood with point-wise convolution, which indirectly improves the neighborhood for generalization.

Inspired by the discussion above, we define a novel convolution operation, PointConvFormer, which takes into account both the relative position pi− p and the feature difference x(pi)− x(p)
The PointConvFormer layer of a point p with its neighbourhood N (p) can be written as: p =w(pi − p) ⊤ψ([x(pi) − x(p), pi − p])x(pi)
The function w(pi − p) learns the weights respect to the relative positions
The function ψ(·) learns to select useful points in the neighborhood, which works similarly to the attention in transformer
The output of ψ(·) modifies the convolutional filter w(pi − p), allowing each neighborhood point to have both positive and negative contributions.

To build deep neural networks for computer vision tasks, bottleneck residual blocks are constructed with PointConvFormer layer as its main component.
The PointConvFormer layer is a main component of the residual blocks as illustrated in Fig. 3.
The input of the residual block consists of input point features X along with coordinates p.
The residual block uses a bottleneck structure, which consists of two branches.
The main branch of the bottleneck structure includes a linear layer, followed by PointConvFormer layer, followed by another linear layer.
The linear layer is followed by the PointConvFormer layer within the bottleneck structure.
